cell_id: 1701.sample_comment,"The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thrown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tube sof the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. cell_id: 2462.sample_comment,"This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. Three major zones ere distinguished. The limit of the Laminaria zone was at a depth of about 10m; its extension was restricted rather by the absence of rock than by the attenuation of light due to depth. The rock gave way to small boulders and a mixture of silty mud and coarse gravel, characterised by large species such as Axinellid sponges, Polymastia and Asterias. In a depth of about 13.5m, this zone merged into a coarse shelly sand and mud mixture with occasional rock outcrops; here sand waves indicated bottom mobility. Flustra was present on the rock as well as Axinellid sponges, while Cerianthus, Corymorpha and Cancer were prominent on the mud. At about 17m depth (point z) stones and boulders began to appear with some mud and shell debris. The stones were characterised by the presence of Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Bugula spp. together with various hydroids such as Nemertesia spp..","This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. cell_id: 1886.sample_comment,"The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. The mobility and lack of hard substrates restricted the fauna to a few species of fish such as Plaice and Brill. Some hermit crabs, Pagurus bernhardus were also present. The landward edge of the bank slopes downwards at an angle of about 30 degrees to a depth of about 25m where boulders and rock outcrops begin to occur. Some tubes of the polychaete Lanice conchilega were present on the slope. Prominent species on the boulders were Nemertesia ramosa, Bugula turbinata, Tealia felina and Flustra foliacea. The latter two species have been recorded in areas of the Menai Strait which are similarly subject to gravel abrasion.","The whole surface must be highly mobile and is thrown up into a series of large sand waves over 1m in height and running at right angles to the shore. cell_id: 728.sample_comment,"This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the idshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. cell_id: 1684.sample_comment,"This site appears to delimit the seawards extent of the Laminaria. Here the low-lying rocky reefs were interspersed among coarse, shelly sand at a depth of about 13m. old_value: The Laminaria forest extended down a bedrock slope to a depth of about 11m although some Laminaria plants occurred down to about 14m. Occasional large boulders were found within the Laminaria forest which sheltered many sponge and hydroid species as well as Corynactis viridis and the tunicate Clavelina lepadiformis. Red algae extended down to about 14m; below this stones and boulders intermixed with silt and shell material were found. The bottom levelled off at about 16m and was mostly of shelly mud. Bryozoans such as Cellaria and Crisia as well as Axinellid sponges were common in the deeper water and Epizoanthus couchii was present even on horizontal silt covered surfaces. Of all the diving sites this one must be considered the most sheltered from both currents and wave-action.

old_value: The Laminaria forest extended down a bedrock slope to a depth of about 11m although some Laminaria plants occurred down to about 14m. Occasional large boulders were found within the Laminaria forest which sheltered many sponge and hydroid species as well as Corynactis viridis and the tunicate Clavelina lepadiformis. Red algae extended down to about 14m; below this stones and boulders intermixed with silt and shell material were found. The bottom levelled off at about 16m and was mostly of shelly mud. Bryozoans such as Cellaria and Crisia as well as Axinellid sponges were common in the deeper water and Epizoanthus couchii was present even on horizontal silt covered surfaces. Of all the diving sites this one must be considered the most sheltered from both currents and wave-action.

Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encmrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus. Shells of the boring bivalve Hiatella as well as burrows of the polychaete Polydora were found in the limestone.","This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community

cell_id: 461.sample_comment,"This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. Three major zones were distinguished. The limit of the Laminaria zone was at a depth of about 10;m its extension was restricted rather by the absence of rock than by the attenuation of light due to depth. The rock gave way to small boulders and a mixture of silty mud and coarse gravel, characterised by large species such as Axinellid sponges, Polymastia and Asterias. In a depth of about 13.5m, this zone merged into a coarse shelly sand and mud mixture with occasional rock outcrops; here sand waves indicated bottom mobility. Flustra was present on the rock as well as Axinellid sponges, while Cerianthus, Corymorpha and Cancer were prominent on the mud. At about 17m depth (point z) stones and boulders began to appear with some mud and shell debris. The stones were characterised by the presence of Alcyonidium gelatinosum and Bugula spp. together with various hydroids such as Nemertesia spp..","This was the only site which consisted mostly of soft bottom material occasionally interrupted by a small rock outcrop. cell_id: 95.sample_comment,"This is a small rocky outcrop frequented by seals and lying about 500m west of Porth Solfach. On the landward side the water was very shallow (about 4m deep) and the Laminaria forest, broken up by patches of sand, extended out to the reef from the main island. Close to the reef on the seaward side the depth was about 10m and although L. hyperborea was present it was not as dense as on the east cpoast. The bedrock was broken up into a series of large gullies, some with small stones and sediment on the bottom. Most of the flat rock surfaces were covered with algae but on vertical faces, and under overhangs, the sponges, hydroids and bryozoans were again common. Nemertesia antennina was rare at this site and no N. ramosa were seen at all. The starfish Marthasterias glacialis was later recorded (A.R. cell_id: 976.sample_comment,"This was a medium-sloping shore with a SE aspect, bearing a mixed Ascophyllum/Fucus/Balanus/Patella community, indicating a `semi-sheltered' condition. Less Fucus was found to the west where the shore becomes steeper. Balanus cover was 50% to 80% on parts of the midshore. Lower down were many pools with encrusting coralline floors. Himanthalia was abundant on open rock and in pools. A limestone band was present similar to that at Maen Du, pitted by Patella and some Hiatella holes containing anemones (Cereus?). On this the cover was mostly Audouinella with encrusting coralline algae and Corallina together with a little Balanus and Fucus.
